California
Fair P01itical
Practices Commission
April 19, 1991
Thonas Tomlinson, Acting Director of Planning
City of San Juan capistrano
32400 Paseo Adelanto
san Juan caPistrano, cA 92575
Re: Your Request for Advice
our File No. I -91- 171
Dear Mr. TomL inson:
Pursuant to our telephone conversation of March 7, 1991, you
have provided additiona] information concerning a proposed
subdivision. Mr. John Shaw, city attorney. and Mr. Dennis Haehn,
city pJ.anning commissioner, had sought advice regarding their
dutlel and responsibilities under the conflict-of-interest
provisions or the Political Reform Act (the 
1 with respect
to decisions regarding a proposed subdivision. Their concerns are
addressed in the Shaw Advice Letters, A-91-125 and A-91-139
(copies enclosed).
rn addition to providing additional information in your
Letter of March L2, I99L, you also requested advice concerning
your own responsibilities, 
as the city's acting director of
pl.anning, under the Act's con f I ict-o f- interest provi'sions. with
respect to the same proPosed subdivision.
The following advice is based upon the facts provided in your
letter of March L2, L99L, in Mr. Shaw's letters of llarch 1, 1991.
and in telephone conversations with you on l*larch 7, 1991 and with
Mr. shaw on March L2, L99L. Your question does not reference a
specific pending governmental decision, therefore, we are treating
ytur r"qrrlst as one for informal assistance.2
I Gor..nr.nt code sections 81ooo-91015. AII statutory references
are to the Government code unless otherwise indicated. 
Comnission
regulations appear at 2 caLifornia code of Regulations section
18ooo, et seo. A11 references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California code of Requlations.
2 
r.,fo.ral assistance does not provide the requestor e/ith the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329 (c) . )
Street, Suite 8OO o P.O. Box 8O7 o Sacramento CA 958O4-O8O7 . (916)322.5660
4281


File No. A-91-171
Page 2
OUEST I ON
A forthcoming decision before the city's plannj-ng commission
concerns a proposed 6O-Iot subdivision rnore than 30o feet and less
than 2,5oo feet from your residence. As acting planning director,
are you permitted to participate in planning commission decisions
concerning the proposed subdivision. including approval of the
subdivision tentative map?
CONCLUS ION
You may participate in decisions regarding the proposed
subdivision provided the decisions will not have a foreseeable
rnaterial financial effect on your property interests which is
distinguishabLe from the effect on the public generally.
FACTS
The city of San Juan Capistrano planning commission is
scheduled to hold its first public hearing on a proposed tentative
subdivision nap for a 60-lot proposed subdivision. 
You are the
city's acting director of Planning, and have indicated that your
residence "fits within the operative l-anguage of FPPc regulation
18702.3(a) (3)." 
l,ie understand this to mean that your residence is
located more than 3OO feet, but less than 2500 feet, from the
ProPosed subdivision 
ANALYS r s
As the city's acting director of planning you are an employee
of a local goveinment agency and are therefore a public official'
(sectj-on 82048.) As a public official 
you are prohibited from
making, participating in, or using your official 
position to
influence a governmental decision in which you knot, or have reason
to know you have a financial interest. 
(section 87100.)
A public official 
has a financial interest in a decision when
it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision wilI have a
materj.al financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the
public generally, on the official, 
a member of official's
immedj.aie family, or on any real property in which the official
has a direct or indirect interest worth one thousand (S1,00o) or
more. (Section 87103 (b) . )
we assume that your property interest in your residence is
worth one thousand dol.Lars or nore. Therefore, you must
disqualify yourself from participatinq in decisions concerning the
proposed subdivisj,on which wiIl have a reasonably foreseeable
matLrial financial effect on you or your real property interests
that is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally.
(Section 87103 (b) . )
Foreseeable tlaterial Financial Effect
The effect of a decision is reasonably foreseeabLe if there
is a substantial likelj.hood that it will occur. certainty is not


File No. A-91- 171
Page 3
requi.red, but an effect that is merely a possibility is not
reJsonably foreseeable. (Downev cares v. Downev cornnunitv
Development Com. (1987) 195 CaI.App.3d 983; In re Thorner (1975)
1 FPPC Ops. 198.) The Act, hoqrever. does seek to prevent even the
appearance of a possibLe conflict of j.nterest. 
(witt v. Morrow
(L977) 7o Cal.App.3d 817, 823.)
It is reasonable to conclude that the development of specific
property areas will have a financial impact on properties in the
irnmediate or adjoining areas of the development. To the extent
that the specific area's property values are enhanced, the value
of the adjoining properties wilL sinilarly 
be enhanced- For that
reason, iL would appear that decisions to perrnit the proPosed
subdivision to go for$rard satisfy the reasonably foreseeable
requirernent of the Act's conflict of interest provisions.
The Cornmission has adopted Regulation L8702.3 (copy enclosed)
in order to determine whether the foreseeable effect of a decision
is material as to real property in which an official 
has an
interest. 
You have indlcated that your property is Iocated within
2,50o feet, but more than 3oo feet, from the site of the proposed
subdivision. Your situation is therefore identical to that
addressed in the Shaw Advice Letter. A-91-139, and we refer you to
that letter for the analysis under ReguLation 18702'3(a) (3)(A) and
(B).
In brief, the effect of a decision concerning the proposed
subdivision is material as to your real property interest if the
decision will affect the fair market value of residence,
positively or negatively, by ten thousand doll'ars (S10,000) or
*o.., o. Lhe reniat value by one thousand dollars (51,000) or more
per 12 month period.
we have not been provided sufficient information to enable,
or other$rise support, a deternrination whether or not the financial'
thresholds wiII- te met. In providing advice, the Commission does
,,oi act as a fact finder,3 and would, therefore. not evaluate the
factual accuracy of such information. However, without such
information our advice must be of a general nature' r
3 As such, the advice Provided in
to the extent that all material and
presented. (In re oqlesbv, (1975)
this letter is aPPIicabIe onJ-Y
correct facts have been
1 FPPC OPS. 77, coPY enclosed. )
4 S"" Moe Advice Letter, A-90-580, copy enclosed'


File No. A-91-171
Page 4
Regulation :-a702.3 (d) provides some factors to consider in
determining the magnitude of the financial impact on your
property . 5
The 'tPublic ceneralLy" Exception
If you conclude that the specified financial impact and other
requisites as provided for in Regulation la7o2.3(a) (3) (A) and (B)
are present, you !.rou1d have a disqualifying conflict. 
Your
participation in decisions concerning the proposed subdivision
would, nevertheless, be pernitted if the effect of the decision on
your otherwise disqualifying interests was not distinguishable
from the effect on the pubtic generally. (Section 87103.)
The financial effect of the planning commission's decisj.on on
your property is not distinguishable frorn the effect on the public
generally when the decision's affect is substantially the same as
it is on all menbers of the public or a significant segment of the
public. (Regulation 18703; Joroensen Advice Letter, No. A-90-017;
In re Leqan (1985) 9 FPPC Ops. 1,'In re owen (L976) 2 FPPC Ops-
77, copies enclosed. ) The "public generally" is comprised of the
entire jurisdiction of the agency in question (In re Leqan,
supra.), in your situation, the entire city. 
You must determine
if the proposed subdivision decision will impact all, or a
significant segment, of the city's residents in substantially the
same manner . 6
5 such factors include the proximity of the proPerties and the
magnitude of the proposed project in relationship to l'{r ' Haehn's
pr6perty, whether it- is reisonably foreseeable that the decision
t., tn" iroposed subdivision will affect the devetopnent potential
or incoire producing potential of ltr- Haehn's proPerty, and whether
it is reaslnably forlseeable that the proposed subdivision will
result in a chaige of the character of the neighborhood including,
but not lj.mited io, the effect on traffic, 
view, privacy,
intensity of use, noise levels, air emissions or sirniLar traits.
6 Th" factors previously discussed in Regulation 18702.3(d) would
be relevant in any consideration of whether the "public generally'r
exception would appJ.y. A group that is large in.nulnber and
heteiogeneous in quaiity cin constitute a significant 
segTment of
tne pu6tic for the purpases of the 'tpublic generally" exception'
(fn re Ferraro (1978) + fppc Ops. 62.) .To assist you in
;;I".t-f"q 
the 'ipublic generally" exception, r have enclosed
copies of other conrnission advile letters which have addressed the
issue. Enclosed are: west Advice Letter, No' A-89-243; qlher
Advice Letter, ,o. O-t[-+29; Flvnn Advice Letter, No' I-88-430'


FiIe No. A-91- 171
Page 5
I hope this ]etter has provided you with some of the guidance
you requested. If you have any further questions regarding this
Iflatter, or wish to supplenent your advice request with additional
information, please contact ne at (916) 322-59OL.
sincerely,
scott Hallabrin
Division
SH:JSR:ken
Enc I osures

sections.conclusion:
You may participate in decisions regarding the proposed
subdivision provided the decisions will not have a foreseeable
rnaterial financial effect on your property interests which is
distinguishabLe from the effect on the public generally.
